data.title: A Drop In Payrolls
data.description:
By David W. Berson, Ph.D.

A drop in nonfarm payrolls – survey volatility or real weakness? Nonfarm payrolls fell by 92,000 in February, compared with market expectations of a rise of around 50,000. Additionally, revisions to the prior two months totaled a drop of 69,000 (which brought December to a decline as well).

The U-3 unemployment rate edged up to 4.4 percent, while the broader U-6 rate slipped to 7.9 percent. Household employment fell by 185,000 (always more volatile than the nonfarm payroll figures given the smaller sample size).

All of this occurred before the run-up in energy prices brought about by the conflict with Iran.

What should we make of this surprisingly weak employment report? Does it represent the start of a weakening labor market? Is it simply volatility in a survey where the standard error (the 90 percent confidence interval) is more than 200,000? Clearly one month’s worth of data makes the answer to these questions uncertain, and we will have to wait for next month’s report to have a better idea. Other data do not corroborate the drop in payrolls – ADP, ISM, and NFIB surveys all were relatively positive with respect to the jobs. And recall that because of demographic and immigration factors, the trend rate of monthly payroll gains is in the 50,000-70,000 range – much lower than the historical average.

Will the Fed respond to this weak report by easing at the March FOMC meeting? Almost certainly not until there is less uncertainty about the underlying path of job growth. But if nonfarm payroll gains continue to be weak in the coming months, the odds of the Fed easing in April and/or June would rise. The Fed is likely to maintain its “watchful waiting” approach to monetary policy until the path of job creation is clearer. Complicating all of this is the spike in energy prices over the past week in response to the conflict in the Middle East. The sooner that conflict ends and energy prices retreat again, the smaller the impact on economic activity and inflation. But if the price hikes are sustained (or worsen), then, as in all negative supply shocks, we should see slower growth and higher inflation. Given the huge gains in U.S. oil and natural gas production in recent decades, the impact of such a supply shock is likely to be much less than in the supply shocks of the past – but it won’t be nothing.

Also released today was the January retail sales report – which showed a drop of 0.2 percent. But almost all of this came from a sharp decline in auto sales. The core measure of retail sales (excluding autos, gasoline, and building materials) rose by 0.3 percent, not a bad figure at all.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Bank Of America: Slipping Back Into The 'Buy' Zone
data.description:
Down around 12%, shares of Bank of America (BAC) are off to a rough start to 2026. Alongside conflict in the Middle East, we can add concerns surrounding private credit, the impact of artificial intelligence on jobs, and tariffs to the list of things that seem to be weighing on the Charlotte-based giant.

I first covered BAC in late 2024. Rating it "Buy," I liked BAC's valuation and the outlook for its interest margin, which had been relatively subdued due to the specifics of its balance sheet.

This pullback is an opportune time to revisit this investment case. Trading for just 12.5x trailing earnings, BAC once again looks cheap in the context of its growth prospects. I reiterate my previous "Buy" rating.

Interest Margin Expansion

BAC has taken a lot of flak in recent years. Among the charges are that it has been too conservative, particularly compared to high-flying peer JPMorgan (JPM), its closest comp. The numbers certainly seem to back that up. Whether we're talking about profitability, growth, or investment returns, JPM has outgunned BAC, and it’s not even close.

BAC's decision during the COVID years to deploy a slug of its non-maturing deposits into long-dated bonds has come to symbolize this criticism. Back in 2019, the bank had $215 billion in held-to-maturity investment securities on its balance sheet, equal to just over 10% of its total interest-earning assets. These figures had risen to $523 billion and 17%, respectively, as of last quarter.

Being as generous as I could, I did say that it may be prudent to adopt a "through-the-cycle" view of all this. Yes, the consensus is that BAC took on a bit too much duration risk in the early 2020s; however, these bonds could essentially act as a hedge for BAC during the easing cycle and beyond, supporting its interest margin and income. Furthermore, it should be noted just how strong BAC's deposit franchise is, particularly in its Consumer Banking unit, where the rate paid on its deposits was just 0.55% last quarter and 0.65% at the peak of the hiking cycle.

Anyway, this "hedge" is playing out. Despite a circa 135 basis points decline in the effective Fed funds rate, BAC's net interest margin has been rising nicely since my opening piece, as has its net interest income ("NII").

The good news: BAC still has a long repricing runway ahead of it. Per the latest earnings call, there'll be around $12-$15 billion in MBSs and residential mortgages rolling off the book each quarter this year.

During 2026, we expect roughly $12 billion to $15 billion in combined mortgage-backed securities and mortgage loans to roll off quarterly, and those will be replaced with new assets at 150 to 200 basis points higher in yield or they'll allow us to pay down expensive short-term debt.

Alastair Borthwick, Executive VP & CFO

As you can see below, this process won't be limited to 2026, either, with management guiding for over $60 billion in annual maturities over the 2027-2031 period.

Whether BAC replaces these low-yielding assets like-for-like, plows them into other assets, or uses them to pay down its more expensive forms of funding, the upshot will be much the same: a wider net interest margin and rising income. Specifically, management is targeting a 2.4% firm-wide net interest margin in the long run, around 32 basis points above its Q4 2025 level, and circa 5%-7% annualized NII growth.

Bottom Line Impact

BAC has an incredibly diverse business. Alongside the net interest income it generated from its loans, securities, and cash, the bank also booked around $10.18 billion in fee income last quarter, up nearly 7% year-on-year.

Why, then, have I focused so narrowly on its interest margin expansion? Two reasons, really. First, margin-driven NII growth doesn't really attract any incremental expense growth, so this revenue largely flows right on down to pre-tax income. That's precisely what BAC has seen recently, with last quarter's revenue growth outpacing cost growth by around 330 basis points. As a result, pre-tax pre-provision profit rose nearly 13% year-on-year. Management expects another 200 basis points of operating leverage in 2026.

Second, unlike a growing loan book, BAC doesn't need to hold more capital against a higher net interest margin. This makes it highly profitable growth. Last year, BAC earned $29.1 billion in net income against roughly $204 billion in average tangible common equity, for a return of 14.2%. This was an improvement of nearly 130 basis points compared to 2024.

These earnings will largely be headed toward shareholders' pockets. Indeed, given its size, resultant growth prospects, solid profitability, and capital cushion, BAC has to spend most of its profits on shareholder remuneration to avoid accumulating surplus capital. It did $8.1 billion in dividends last year and $21.4 billion in buybacks. Per above, the latter resulted in a 5.2% decline in BAC's share count on a period-end basis, boosting its share-level bottom line beyond its net income growth.

Shares Look Like A Bargain

BAC trades for $47.94 as of this writing, equal to 12.5x last year's earnings per share. This is a little below its ten-year average, as shown below.

I find that interesting given the outlook for BAC's profits. The short version: driven by the operating leverage outlined in the last section, BAC's bottom line is going to swell considerably in the coming years. Consensus has 2028 net income exceeding $36 billion, around 25% higher than last year's print.

More profits will mean more cash for buybacks. This will be a tailwind for earnings per share, which is expected to increase to over $5.50 in the next few years, equating to a 13% annualized rate of growth. Coupled with BAC's 2.3% dividend yield, this gets today's buyer to a circa 15% annualized return.

Being a bank, there are plenty of risks to consider here. Per below, BAC's net loan losses have actually been heading in the right direction, but this could turn if the economy heads south, forcing BAC to boost its provisions. This would eat into its profits.

Above the provisioning line, BAC's revenue could face a few headwinds. For example, its fee income is sensitive to many things, including capital market levels. While fees rose 7% last quarter, over 75% of this growth came from asset management fees within BAC's Wealth segment. At the risk of stating the obvious, this business generates a lot of asset-based revenue. Accordingly, it has been a major beneficiary of elevated stock markets, which have delivered three straight years of double-digit returns. A pullback would weigh on this unit's results. Finally, a sharp fall in interest rates would naturally derail the whole fixed-asset repricing thesis, weighing on BAC's net interest revenue.

These are all risks that prospective investors will need to mull over. However, with BAC offering a mid-teens annualized return from its business and with shares currently trading below their long-run trailing P/E ratio, I think these risks are worth taking on. I rate shares "Buy."

data.title: Petrobras Q4 Earnings: Dividend Yield Higher Than P/E
data.description:
Petróleo Brasileiro: ~7% FWD Yield Meets 5.5x FWD P/E

I last analyzed Petrobras (NYSE:PBR) stock on Jan 29 with an article titled “Venezuela Catalysts Can Benefit Suncor Energy More Than Petrobras.” It focused on the developments in Venezuela and rated PBR as a hold. Since then, a few new developments have emerged in terms of macroeconomics (in particular the Iran conflict and its impact on global oil prices) and PBR’s specific financials, announced in the last Q4 2025 earnings report (ER), as shown in the snapshot below.

With heightened geopolitical risks, I feel it’s especially timely to focus on the return vector that involves the least degree of uncertainty. The vector with the least ambiguity is dividend in my book. As such, this article will focus on the changes in PBR’s dividends and, more importantly, my forward view for future payouts. In the end, my assessment is that the lucrative dividend, especially when combined with low P/E, provides such a thick cushion and makes the stock a solid hold amid global oil uncertainties. This high-yield and low-P/E combination is so extreme that it appears absurd to me. As detailed next, the stock's FWD dividend hovers around 7%, which is higher than its FWD P/E of 5.5x by a good gap (that is, if you only consider the numerical values without the percentage sign).

PBR Stock: Latest Dividend Announcement

In tandem with the ER, the PBR board just approved the latest dividend payout. The approval brought the full-year payout to R$41.2 (with more details quoted below). At the current FX rate, this translates into about $7.77 billion US dollars.

TipRanks News (Mar 6, 2026): On March 5, 2026, Petrobras’ board approved a proposal to submit to the April 16, 2026, Annual General Meeting a distribution of R$ 8.1 billion in shareholder remuneration for the fourth quarter of 2025, bringing total 2025 payouts to R$ 41.2 billion after Selic-rate adjustments and deductions. The remuneration aligns with policy to distribute 45% of free cash flow, with payments in May and June 2026.

As such, even at the stock’s current market capitalization of around $113 billion, which is around the 52-week high, the dividend yield is about 6.9%. With the average market capitalization of $82.3 billion in the past year, the annualized yield is close to 9.5%.

PBR Stock: Dividend Outlook

Looking ahead, the dividend payouts are well covered and have good potential to increase further in my model. With the Q4 ER, total capex totaled US$18.6 billion on a TTM basis, as shown in the cash flow statement below. This is a sizable increase of more than 44% over the 2024 level. Judging by the updates provided in the ER, the company kept making progress on the FPSO P-78 construction and explorations of new assets, particularly in the Equatorial Margin.

Despite the surges in capex, free cash flow (FCF) remains robust and the dividends well covered. As shown in the statement above, cash from operations totaled more than $36.3 billion, resulting in an FCF of around $17.6 billion as of TTM (or $2.72 per ADR). The dividend budget of $7.77 billion thus represents about 44% of the total FCF, well aligned with the company’s goal of distributing 45% of its FCF as dividends, as aforementioned.

Looking ahead, I expect the surges in capital investments to be more tempered and the past capex investments to start contributing. The divestitures of non-core assets could also help release some capex pressure. For example, in the past quarter, PBR completed the auction of the uncontracted areas in Mero and Atapu. All told, my estimates point to an FCF of around $3.2 per ADR. With an FCF payout ratio of 45%, this estimate further translates into a dividend of $1.44 per ADR and a dividend yield of more than 8.3% at the share price as of this writing.

Other Risks And Final Thoughts

In terms of downside risks, PBR entails all the risks generic to other oil companies, such as sensitivity to oil prices and geopolitical conflicts. Here, I will focus on a few risks that are more unique to PBR and the analysis presented above. Unlike the US oil stocks most energy investors are familiar with (the likes of ExxonMobil or Chevron), Petrobras is a state-controlled entity. The Brazilian government is the majority shareholder and possesses golden shares that allow it to directly influence company leadership and strategy. This control may not always align with shareholders’ interests, and the company has suffered frequent leadership turnover in recent years.

Also, in all my above analysis, I used the current BRL (Brazilian Real) to USD exchange rate of 1 BRL to 0.19 USD, as shown in the chart below. However, as you can also see from the chart, the BRL-USD exchange rates have fluctuated considerably in recent years. Between the two years of 2023 and 2025, the FX rate changes from a peak of about 0.21 to a trough of 0.16, a change of more than 30%, quite extreme for a major currency.

There are a few fundamental reasons behind such large fluctuations. As a major exporter of iron ore, oil, and agricultural products, the BRL has a strong flavor of a commodity currency in my mind. Fluctuations in global demand for these resources directly impact the BRL. For example, the decline in the chart during late 2024 often correlates with periods of cooling global industrial demand or shifts in energy prices. In the meantime, Brazil has been faced with elevated inflation in recent years (and is still facing the issue in my view). To fight inflation, the Brazil central bank has maintained relatively high-interest rates. A higher interest rate tends to encourage foreign carry trade investors. However, the relative attractiveness of trade is dynamic and depends on how central banks in other countries (i.e., the US Federal Reserve) set their interest rates. As these dynamics shift, the BRL-USD FX rate could continue experiencing high volatilities.

To close, this PBR revisit is motivated by the ongoing geopolitical conflicts, its Q4 ER, and also the latest dividend announcements. These changes have made me feel it is especially timely for investors to focus on dividends, the return vector that involves the least degree of uncertainty. Admittedly, FX rate change still represents a sizable uncertainty (as much as 30% in recent years). However, even with a 30% margin of error, I feel the core thesis should still stand, which is that the combination of high yield and low P/E makes the stock a solid hold despite all the downsides.

data.title: IMOM: Better Tactical ETF Than Long-Term Holding
data.description:
IMOM Strategy

Alpha Architect Intl Quantitative Momentum ETF (IMOM) is an actively managed fund launched on 12/22/2015, with an objective of long-term capital appreciation. IMOM has a portfolio of 50 stocks, a 30-day SEC yield of 1.44%, and an expense ratio of 0.38%. Distributions are paid annually. The fund is managed by Dr. Wesley Gray and Dr. Jack Vogel, authors of the book Quantitative Momentum (Wiley).

As described in the prospectus by Alpha Architect, the methodology starts from the largest 1,500 stocks by market capitalization listed in countries included in the MSCI EAFE Index, then reduces the investing universe to the 500 with the highest total returns over the past 12 months, excluding the most recent month. A quantitative, rules-based process identifies highly liquid stocks with the most consistent positive returns during the trailing 12 months. Finally, the top 50 to 200 momentum stocks are included as constituents.

The portfolio turnover is high: 411% in the most recent fiscal year. This article will use as a benchmark the MSCI EAFE Index, represented by iShares MSCI EAFE ETF (EFA).

Portfolio

IMOM has 64% of asset value in large- and mega-cap companies and 36% in mid-caps. The portfolio has a focus on Japan (34.9%) and notable exposure in Germany (17.6%). No other country weighs more than 10%. Compared to the benchmark, IMOM mostly overweights Japan, Germany, and Israel, while it greatly downplays the U.K., France, and Switzerland.

The fund is concentrated in three sectors: industrials (32%), financials (21.2%), and materials (20.1%). Other sectors are below 6%. The sector breakdown is not typical of the strategy; it may change with sector relative momentum.

The portfolio is diversified, with low company-specific risk. The top 10 holdings, listed in the next table, represent 22.1% of asset value, and the largest position weighs 2.5%. Nonetheless, IMOM is more concentrated than EFA, where the top 10 names weigh only 13.6%.

Fundamentals

IMOM is classified by Morningstar in the “Foreign Large Blend” ETF category, but it shows a strong inclination to growth. Indeed, it has significantly higher aggregate growth rates compared to EFA (especially cash flow growth), as reported in the next table. Additionally, its valuation ratios are close to the benchmark, unlike most growth funds. In summary, IMOM has solid growth characteristics without sacrificing value.

IMOM has the narrowest portfolio and the highest turnover. It is the best performer over the past year but comes in last position for total return and Sharpe ratio since November 2017. Additionally, it has the highest risk metrics. In this time frame, IDMO leads the pack by a wide margin.

Takeaway

Alpha Architect Intl Quantitative Momentum ETF has a high-conviction portfolio of 50 stocks with high and steady returns over the past 12 months. IMOM has strong growth characteristics without sacrificing value. Company-specific risk is low, but the portfolio is heavily weighted in Japan and industrials. IMOM has underperformed the benchmark EFA and momentum ETF peers in the long term but has outperformed them over the past year. Due to its excellent performance in short periods of time and high volatility, IMOM is best suited for tactical allocation. Based on historical performance and risk metrics, IDMO looks like a better alternative for long-term international exposure in the momentum factor.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: My Largest BDC Pick KBDC Has Again Proven The Skeptics Wrong
data.description:
I have made Kayne Anderson BDC, Inc. (KBDC) my largest BDC position almost since the very first moment when I saw and assessed its case, which dates back to October 2024.

See Kayne Anderson BDC: Close To Being My Favorite Defensive BDC Pick - the first bullish article on KBDC on this platform (issued by me) that clearly highlights my rationale to go long here.

Even though before the release of the Q4 2025 earnings report and the SaaS-driven drama I circulated a less bullish piece, I still made it clear that KBDC remains a defensive pick that should be considered by durable income investors. It was just that my position in KBDC had become disproportionate, and given the recent outperformance, I decided to redirect KBDC's dividends elsewhere.

This is how KBDC has performed over the past month or so:

It is quite obvious that some part of the market has recognized the underlying value and defense that KBDC brings to the table, especially in the context of SaaS fears. KBDC's alpha over the overall BDC sector (BIZD) is indeed impressive.

Now, after digesting the Q4 earnings report, I have to say that all of my core thesis items on KBDC are working out just as I thought. It is definitely a good thing, as it provides yet another base of evidence for those investors who have been doubting KBDC to really consider integrating this name into their high-quality BDC portfolio.

Let me explain.

Thesis Review

There have been only a handful of BDCs that have reported positive quarter-to-quarter NII per share movement.

The reasons for that are the following and exactly in the order of what I've listed here:

  • Headwinds from three interest rate cuts in late 2024.
  • New and existing loan investment financings at tighter spreads.
  • Non-accruals (not a system-wide issue, at least not yet).

Because of these aspects, BDCs have suffered yet another quarter with a negative rate of change on the NII per share front.

This has in turn triggered several dividend cuts in the system and made the overall sector average base dividend coverage go from bad to worse - i.e., currently at 99%.

KBDC is, however, a different BDC or a BDC that has placed itself in a different and more favorable spot. So, just like Trinity Capital Inc. (TRIN), which has also been my Top 3 BDC pick for quite some time, KBDC has managed to not only preserve the NII per share metric but also grow it.

The NII per share came in at $0.44, which is $0.01 per share above the previous quarterly result. And it was really a combination of debt-funded portfolio growth, continued rotation out of the broadly syndicated loan area, and some tailwinds from slightly reduced incentive fees.

As a result of this, KBDC's base dividend coverage stands at 110%, which is a level that offers two things: 1) room to absorb potential shocks and 2) access to retained/internal equity capital to grow the portfolio.

It is not a surprise that the Co-CEO, Kenneth Leonard, sent the following message to the market in the Q4 earnings call:

I would like to add that based on our current view of the market and our portfolio, we expect to be able to pay the $0.40 dividend for the entirety of 2026.

If we speak about the underlying portfolio and credit quality, then the messages are just as strong.

The non-accrual rate remained unchanged at a very low level of 1.4% (on an FV basis). The unrealized loss came in at 0.6% of NAV, which was related to prudent markdowns, reflecting an increasingly volatile environment, but not distress that would lead to inflated non-accruals.

The performing portfolio investments continued to enjoy one of the most defensive properties in the entire BDC arena: e.g., the weighted average leverage and the interest coverage were at 4.5x and 2.4x, respectively. The LTV was about 43%, while typically in the sector we can observe a metric that comes closer to 50%.

More importantly, KBDC has clearly benefited from the value-based lending approach, which has inherently deemphasized the SaaS component in the loan investment mix. This is what the Top 10 sector/industry split looks like for KBDC:

We don't see anything from the SaaS world in the Top 10 mix. In fact, if we dissect the 10-K filing, we will see that the exposure to SaaS is about 2%.

For most sector peers, including names that are commonly viewed as best of breed, the concentration in SaaS is certainly above 10% and, in many instances, even slightly above 20%.

So, what we have here is a BDC that is strong on both the NII generation and portfolio quality sides.

The question could be whether KBDC's dividend, which currently sits at 11.5%, is sustainable. If we for a moment ignore what the CEO said and look at the key financial dynamics, I would even argue that the dividend coverage should improve as we move forward.

The key driver for this kind of assumption is below-average debt level, which as of Q4, 2025, was 1.02x. The long-term sweet spot or target level is closer to 1.20x, which is also consistent with the sector average.

As KBDC continues to ramp up the leverage until it hits optimum and reinvests (or buy back shares) the undistributed NII, the total income per share should rise accordingly. This is per definition accretive to the NII per share generation, especially knowing that the incentive fees are on a decline (mostly due to no notable unrealized/realized gains) and debt financing spreads continue to drop.

For example, during 2025, KBDC increased its debt to equity from 0.86x to 1.02x, which has translated to an NII per share increase of $0.04 despite considerable pressures from tighter spreads, lower base rates, and minor non-accruals.

It is unlikely to see so many cuts as in 2025. And when it comes to spreads, we will most likely be observing the opposite trend - i.e., the widening of spreads as the sector has become increasingly nervous, and struggling BDCs could be forced to offload some of their loans to investors who have maintained more disciplined underwriting and have sufficient capital (like KBDC).

The Bottom Line

KBDC is a unique BDC.

It has one of the most defensive portfolios in the BDC game that is insulated from SaaS risks. The current dividend coverage looks very strong as well, where there is a solid ground for actually expecting improving coverage going forward.

These are truly timely features that should support dividend and NAV stability.

At a P/NAV of 0.85x and a dividend yield of 11.5%, KBDC is a buy. It is one of the most defensive and SaaS-neutralized BDCs one can buy right now.

Finally, when it comes to my own capital allocation in relation to KBDC, I continue to maintain it as the largest BDC allocation in the portfolio.

But given the exposure that I already have to KBDC and the abundance of other high-quality, first-lien-focused, and even lower-priced BDC opportunities such as Blue Owl Capital (OBDC) and Morgan Stanley Direct Lending Fund (MSDL), I continue to reallocate KBDC's dividends elsewhere.

data.title: Braze Lacks Operating Progress As GenAI Hollowing Out Threatens (Downgrade)
data.description:
Investment Outlook

Braze, Inc.’s (BRZE) stock has sold off in recent months along with the wider software sector on fears of generative AI disruption.

I previously analyzed BRZE in November 2022 with a Hold outlook due to worsening operating losses.

The company hasn’t made any progress on its high operating losses, isn’t shareholder-friendly in its capital allocation choices, and has some risk of disruption from GenAI technologies.

Given its lack of operating progress and other issues, my outlook on Braze is a bearish Sell.

Braze’s Market And Approach

Braze provides clients with a cloud-based customer communications and engagement platform via a SaaS revenue model.

The company's system offers customers numerous channels to message their prospects, clients and other parties to maximize communication and collaboration.

According to a market research report prepared by Grand View Research in 2024, the customer engagement solutions market was an estimated $23.4 billion in 2023 and was expected to exceed $50 billion by 2030.

If achieved, this growth rate would represent a CAGR of 11.8% from 2024 to 2030, a fairly robust rate of growth for an already large market size.

By region, North America held the highest market share in 2023 at 38.2%.

The recent introduction of advanced analytics and generative AI technologies has enhanced offerings to clients just as they seek more effective tools for maximizing prospect interest and customer loyalty.

The chart below shows the historical and expected growth trajectory of the US customer engagement solutions market through 2030, divided between solutions and services:

Competitors in the customer engagement solutions market include:

  • Adobe (ADBE)

  • Klaviyo (KVYO)

  • Calabrio

  • Genesys

  • IBM (IBM)

  • NICE

  • Hubspot (HUBS)

  • Twilio (TWLO)

  • Oracle (ORCL)

  • Pegasystems (PEGA)

  • Salesforce (CRM)

  • SAP SE (SAP)

  • Sprinklr (CXM) - partial overlap

  • Iterable

  • Other point solutions.

Braze’s strengths include its multi-channel capabilities, open ecosystem that helps with client retention and increasing focus on AI-native decision support with its 2025 OfferFit acquisition.

Weaknesses include dependency on discretionary marketing and digital engagement budgets, and shorter contract durations.

An opportunity for BRZE includes using AI to shift from execution to outcome by selling personalization, optimization and autonomous journey decision-making.

Braze is threatened by not only intense direct competitors, but also adjacent players adding the customer communication or engagement pieces to their bundles. It is also exposed to generative AI disruption in content creation, but not in deterministic infrastructure capabilities.

Recent Financial Trends

Topline revenue (blue columns) rose sequentially and year-over-year on existing customer expansion, renewals and new business across verticals and geographies, while operating losses remained heavy on lower gross margin.

The company's core operating leverage ratio continued to decline, a negative trend indicating less operating efficiency for each dollar of revenue growth:

Gross margin (blue line) fell due to higher hosting costs and messaging volumes, indicating concerning unit economics trends, while SG&A as a percentage of revenue (yellow line) moved lower on cost improvements, and R&D (purple) remained sequentially flat.

Earnings per share continued to make no progress toward breakeven and remains heavily negative with no obvious path to profitability.

The table below shows various forward and trailing twelve-month operating metrics and financial figures:

Metric

Amount

EV/Sales - FWD

2.6

EV/EBITDA - FWD

49.1

Rev. Growth Rate Est. - FWD

22.1%

FY 2026 FWD EPS Estimate

$0.42

Price/Sales - TTM

3.0

P/E GAAP - TTM

NM

Revenue Growth Rate - TTM

23.0%

Net Income Margin - TTM

-16.9%

EBITDA Margin - TTM

-16.5%

Market Capitalization

$2,180,000,000

Enterprise Value

$1,880,000,000

Operating Cash Flow

$69,150,000

EPS (Fully Diluted)

-$1.10

Free Cash Flow per Share - TTM

$0.56

Seeking Alpha Quant Score

Hold: 2.92

(Source - Seeking Alpha.)

The company's GAAP Rule of 40 results have been uneven and poor, as the chart shows here:

Capital Allocation

At the end of FQ3 2026, Braze had $383.3 million in cash, equivalents and short-term investments and no debt, so a strong balance sheet.

For the twelve months ended October 31, 2025, Braze generated free cash flow of $63.2 million after $5.9 million in capital expenditures.

However, SBC (Stock-based compensation) was a very high $134 million for the period, so free cash flow net of SBC was a negative ($70.8 million).

Also, net stock issuance for the trailing twelve-month period was $15.3 million, so the company is diluting shareholders through both hefty SBC awards and new stock issuance.

The company closed on its acquisition of OfferFit in June of 2025 for $325 million and paid for it with $195.3 million in cash and $108.7 million in Class A stock, which contributed to the increase in outstanding shares during the trailing twelve-month period.

So, Braze’s SBC intensity is higher than comparable SaaS firms at a similar scale and stage of development.

Why I’m Negative On Braze

Braze continues to grow revenue in the low 20s percent per year, although it also continues to produce high operating losses and is making no progress toward breakeven.

The firm is also pursuing shareholder-unfriendly capital allocation policies, since its high SBC does not appear to be producing meaningful shareholder value for its cost.

BRZE is navigating an increasingly complex environment with respect to generative AI technologies.

It is seeking to both incorporate new capabilities into its platform while also realizing that a certain subset of its customers will build their own integrations alongside Braze, but using its APIs.

One concern is to what degree this client behavior, which is now likely limited to their more sophisticated clients, will expand out to its broader client base, potentially reducing the value add of its platform functionality, outside of its APIs.

This is a potential issue for many software firms across the broader landscape, that of being "hollowed out" by GenAI capabilities.

While the core infrastructure layer of its functions is likely not very vulnerable to disruption from below (GenAI), content generation, campaign optimization and segmentation are subject to disruption, so I rate Braze’s overall vulnerability to GenAI displacement as moderate.

In response, management’s growth strategies include building out a GenAI layer on top of its engagement platform with its OfferFit acquisition, allowing it to win more enterprise customers.

It is also using a partnership with Shopify for its increasing focus on eCommerce markets.

The graphic below shows the frequency of various negative-oriented keywords in the most recent conference call between management and analysts.

The chart shows that Braze is continuing to see challenges with longer and slower enterprise replacement cycles.

As to valuation, the stock market is valuing BRZE at an EV/Sales multiple of around 2.6x on an estimated next twelve months revenue growth rate of around 22% against a median Meritech Capital SaaS Index implied ARR growth rate of approximately 15% (Source).

And the index shows a recent median Net Dollar Retention Rate of 106% vs. Braze’s overall NRR of 108%.

The index of publicly held SaaS application software companies showed a forward EV/Revenue multiple median of around 3.3x on March 3, 2026.

So, by comparison, Braze is currently being valued by public investors at a discount to the broader index as of March 3, 2026, despite a higher forward revenue growth rate estimate and a higher net dollar retention rate.

This is likely due to the company's history of high operating losses, which, in a higher interest rate environment, are penalized by investors to a greater degree than companies at operating breakeven or better.

Add to that the recent selloff in software company stocks on the general narrative surrounding fear of disruption risks from generative AI.

In sum, Braze’s low valuation appears justified due to its lack of meaningful progress toward operating breakeven, an unfavorable capital allocation approach for shareholders and potential for GenAI hollowing out of its platform over time.

So, my outlook on BRZE is a bearish Sell ahead of their earnings on March 24th.

data.title: Ulta Beauty: Quarterly Preview And The Case For A Premium
data.description:
Introduction

Since my last article, "Ulta Beauty: High Cost Today, Much More Robust Tomorrow," Ulta Beauty, Inc.'s (ULTA) stock price has gone up approximately 8%, which is evidence that the market reaction broadly confirmed the scenario I outlined in my previous article.

The major retail investors are apparently fearful of profit-taking when earnings will be released on March 12, 2026 (after hours). However, the stock’s price action suggests steady institutional demand following the results. Earnings releases always produce volatility in the stock, as indicated by options data, and according to Bloomberg, the stock could go up (or go down) 6.7% following the earnings release. Options volume has also been increasing at a rate of 52% since the 2,943 options were traded during the week prior to the last earnings report. A put-to-call ratio of 0.5 indicates that a majority of options traders have a positive perception of the company.

I am not forecasting anything here; the possibility of anything happening is endless, and I don't have a crystal ball. However, I do have a developed investment thesis based on a fundamental and misunderstood concept. While the company is accepting reduced margins now to strengthen its long-term competitive position, I will provide additional information on this in my full analysis below.

A Strong Customer Base

Ulta Beauty Inc., currently the largest retailer of cosmetics in the U.S., operates in a differentiated competitive position, combining mass and prestige beauty in a single retail platform. The beauty company’s business model is both very successful and quite different from all competitors. The company is able to sell the same product lines as Walgreens or CVS (mass-market products) and also carry the same luxury brands that are found in department stores. No other retailer carries the same level of brand offerings in a single location across the nation. Ulta’s most important competitive advantage is its large and loyal customer base. Over 46.3 million consumers use the company’s loyalty rewards program, and over 95 percent of the company’s sales come from these active users.

As I discussed previously, the company is using its large customer base to collect massive amounts of data that allow it to dramatically lower its acquisition cost by targeting new customers with advertising and not having to provide them with discounts that would erode profit margins.

In addition, the company is transforming its physical footprint in North America. In August 2026, ULTA will terminate its historical agreement with Target Corporation (TGT). After the termination of this agreement, the company is expected to focus more on high-margin standalone store locations. The company desires to increase traffic in its stores without sharing profit margins with the intermediary (Target) that has been a part of ULTA's history.

At the same time, the company is rapidly expanding its international footprint through the acquisition of British-based retailer Space NK. The acquisition includes 83 stores in the UK and Ireland and will be led by original CEO Andy Lightfoot to ensure complete continuity of leadership. The acquisition of Space NK will be complementary to the recent direct openings of stores in Mexico and the Middle East. Specifically, the opening of a store in the Mall of the Emirates in Dubai is an important step in the expansion into foreign markets. The company is now able to expand beyond its reliance on the domestic U.S. market.

Quarterly Preview

Analysts forecast revenue of $3.81B and earnings of around $7.98.

Comparable store sales were expected to increase by 2.5-3.5 percent as per the company's guidance for the comparable store sales metric; however, this was revised upward to 4.4-4.7 percent.

Based on total data, I am expecting much larger increases in comparable store sales than that indicated by the bears. In fact, JPMorgan analysts, in a recent research report, estimate that there is the potential for a 6 percent increase in comparable store sales. The fragrance segment has continued to generate strong volume growth. Omnichannel traffic during the holidays has exceeded the bears' preliminary estimates.

What am I looking at in the numbers in this much-anticipated quarterly earnings report? I'm looking for detailed expense control. Operating Expenses increased to 29.4% in the 3rd quarter, 250 bps above last year.

Digital Infrastructure & Space NK costs are around $24M per quarter. The institutional investors are calling for a return to a very disciplined financial approach immediately. Yes, there will be "one-time expenses," however, they continue to have a negative impact on the quarter. However, the 3rd quarter gross margin was 40.4%, 120 bps better than estimates. So, it is clear the company has absolute pricing power regardless of what is happening with inflation. Store loss and Shrinkage are starting to decrease due to new procedures.

The upcoming quarterly earnings report will therefore be particularly important. I think the chief executive officer (CEO) will report sales above expectations while reporting an operating margin that is nearly unchanged. There is one other major economic fact that is being ignored by many of those making these projections: the "One Big Beautiful Bill," as it has been called. This bill, which changes the face of taxation, will have all of the deductions for tips and overtime, which, in 2026, will result in a significant increase in tax refunds. Essentially, it's a kind of cash injection into discretionary spending. High-end retail businesses will benefit directly, quickly, and significantly.

Valuation

The price ratios as they stand at present might be quite off-putting for the individual investors. The firm is currently trading at a forward non-GAAP price-earnings ratio of about 25.53 times next year's earnings, which is quite high, as the average P/E for the Consumer Discretionary Sector is only 15.57 times. Compared to 9.74 times for the Consumer Discretionary Sector, the five-year forward Enterprise Value to Earnings Before Interest and Taxes (EV/EBITDA) multiple is about 17.12 times. The Price-to-Book Ratio for the next five years is about 10.57 times, while the lowest ratio for the overall market was 2.25 times. As such, at initial review this would appear to be an overvalued or at least an equity that does not provide much comfort in terms of value. But appearance is deceiving to those who only analyze the surface of the data.

The table allows for an easy comparison of multiple (price-to-earnings) values for ULTA with those of its direct competitor Sally Beauty Holdings (SBH), as well as the median of the retail sector.

METRIC ULTA SBH SECTOR
P/E FORWARD 25.5x 7.4x 16.8x
EV/EBIT Forward 17.1x 6.4x 9.7x
Price/Book Value 10.5x 1.7x 2.2x

In essence, investors are paying a long-term premium on the market for the extreme quality of this business. As the company expands into future earnings, we can expect to see the price-to-earnings ratio of the company come down to historically extremely attractive levels. In 2027, the price-to-earnings ratio of the company is expected to be 22.94x. Additionally, the price-to-earnings ratio of the company is expected to decline even more in 2028 to 20.77x. This geometric reduction in the multiple paid for shares of stock is supported by the company’s fundamentals.

It should also be noted that the company generates a large free cash flow yield, which it uses to fund its very large share buyback program, and that historically, the company has maintained a high rate of return on equity of approximately 50%.

I use a 23x multiple for my estimate of 2027 earnings, which results in a fair market price of approximately $800; I believe the strong characteristics of the business, as well as the high level of repeat customer purchases, provide a solid basis for this "quality" premium. Therefore, comparing to Sally Beauty Holdings, etc., which is of lower quality than Ulta Beauty, becomes irrelevant since you do not create wealth over time by purchasing low-quality assets at a discount.

Risks

Clearly, as the first basic risk, I put macroeconomic factors at the top of the list because any macroeconomic slowdown would have a negative impact on companies like Ulta Beauty. For example, a significant drop in discretionary spending would translate into reduced traffic in many physical stores. Specifically, the company is suffering from operational deleveraging, which clearly carries high risks. As a growing company with a rather complex and rigid cost structure, organic growth of at least 3% is required to maintain operating margins; otherwise, it risks losing money. Growth below the three percent threshold that maintains these margins will cause profit to quickly drop off. Additionally, any actions taken by competitors (like Sephora) should also be carefully monitored.

In order to successfully integrate the recently acquired Space NK asset in the UK, there exist numerous risks to both operations and culture. The wrong move with the highly sensitive European expansion of such an important brand could rapidly consume all of the hard-earned money that has been accumulating over years.

However, long-term planning opportunity greatly exceeds short-term uncertainty. The "Replenish & Save" subscription program that the company is introducing will establish a high margin and very predictable source of recurring revenue. The company will also launch aggressive marketing campaigns by partnering with professional athletes to promote their product. The current trend of young demographics and Gen Z is driving a super cycle of consumption. In contrast to the trends of 2016, where it was all about skin care, color cosmetics are now leading the charge in terms of sales (color cosmetics have become a totally unrelenting category for new generations of consumers to spend money on).

Conclusion

My confidence in this real retail leader is strong today. Heavy lines cost today is literally financing tomorrow's technological infrastructure. The visibility of its future earnings combined with its substantial pricing power provides a premium to trade at higher than current market multiples. It has a solid balance sheet that will protect it from shock to the global economy; therefore, I am making my recommendation a Buy and raising my price target to $800, as previously discussed.

data.title: What The Middle East Conflict Means For Canada's Markets
data.description:
Conflict in the Middle East has been weighing on markets, but Michael O’Brien, Co-Head of Canadian Equity Portfolio Management with TD Asset Management, says the TSX may be well positioned to weather the uncertainty.

Transcript

Greg Bonnell: Investors are facing plenty of uncertainty with the conflict in the Middle East. But what does it mean for Canadian markets? Joining us now to discuss is Michael O'Brien, managing director and co-head of Canadian equity portfolio management with TD Asset Management. Michael, always great to have you on the program.

Michael O'Brien: Yeah. It's nice to be back. No shortage of topics.

Greg Bonnell: You weren't even scheduled to be with us this week. But of course, we have a lot to talk about. It's several days now into what we're seeing in the Middle East. What does it mean for Canadian equities?

Michael O'Brien: Well, I think the biggest focus for investors globally, obviously, from this conflict is the impact on energy prices, specifically oil, but also natural gas. I'm sure the viewers have heard this ad nauseam for the last week, but 20% of the world's oil transits through the Strait of Hormuz, roughly a similar amount of LNG, which is critical for European and Asian buyers. Liquefied natural gas is also going through those same waterways.

With the disruptions we've seen, it's raised a lot of questions. And so really, that's the primary focus or transmission mechanism for investors in terms of trying to handicap where we're at -- is the impact on energy prices. And obviously, we've seen a very noticeable spike in oil prices, similarly for LNG cargoes. So that's the global focus.

The good news for Canada -- let's start by saying, obviously, higher energy prices -- that's a tax on consumption. It adds an inflationary element at a time when inflation is still pretty sticky. So all else equal -- not a great thing. However, when you look at it -- at the North American or Canadian perspective, North America is an energy superpower, both the US and Canada. And so in that respect, we're not reliant on the oil getting through the Strait of Hormuz. That's more of a problem for the Europeans and folks in Asia.

And so in that respect, Canada is in a better spot than most. Really, the critical thing that we'll all be watching, aside from just the humanitarian aspect of this -- it's more from an economic perspective. How persistent are these supply disruptions? How long is the Strait of Hormuz closed? How lasting is this spike in energy prices?

Because if it's a relatively short-term thing, if it's a four or five-week thing, military operations start to wind down, traffic begins flowing through the Strait of Hormuz -- probably not a significant impact on the overall macro, on inflation, on oil prices. However, if it drags on longer than that, the consequences or the impacts on the real economy will get worse.

Greg Bonnell: Is that what's making it hard right now for the markets just try to price where they want to be on this? I think in terms of geopolitical conflict in the Middle East, it supports, or it should support, the price of oil, and it has, should support the price of gold -- some days yes, some days no. These are things that the Canadian market has right now. It's been a lot of fluctuation in a short period of time. Is the market still trying to figure out where all this is headed?

Michael O'Brien: Well, absolutely, the market's trying to figure-- you can see this every day. We're, what, four days into the war going hot, a shooting war starting? And I think each day, it's been back or forth. So clearly, investors are struggling to figure out where this is all going and, probably more important, how it's going to end. I think that in the very short term, it's pretty obvious that the US and the Israelis just have overwhelming military superiority. But where it goes from here is still very murky.

So again, bringing it back to Canada, we are in a relatively advantaged position. Energy is the easiest thing to talk to, obviously. And energy is about 15%, 16% of the TSX Composite. It's a positive for our oil and gas sector. That's a big chunk of the TSX.

Like you say, things like gold, which -- gold hasn't been acting well the last few days -- but through this process, gold stocks, I would rather have a lot of exposure to these commodities, which are inflation hedges, than not having them. I think you're going to see a -- well, you've already seen a fairly significant uptick in bond yields. So interest rates are going up, again, anticipating more of an inflationary or reflationary impact.

If you think about what that does for the different sectors of various stock markets, again -- so Canada, 16% is energy stocks. Net positive. The materials space tends to be an inflation hedge, or does well in a reflationary environment. That's another 20% of the Canadian market.

Within reason, if interest rates are rising and yield curves are steepening without a big impact on the macro, that's a very positive thing for our banks and our insurers. That's another 30-plus percent of the market. So we're talking 70% type of the market in Canada that's relatively better positioned to absorb, or even sometimes profit from, this sort of pro-inflation type of impulse that we're seeing through the economy right now.

Greg Bonnell: So that's the markets. We talked a little bit about the economy overall. But as we take a look at what's happening right now, and I said we can't figure out how the duration is going to be, what the outcome is going to be, how is the Canadian economy positioned?

Michael O'Brien: So the Canadian economy right now -- I would say we're in OK position. The growth in the back half of the year was consistent with, I think, where the Bank of Canada expected it to be. So I would say it's OK.

Clearly, we're seeing two different economies right now, where the more resource-based economy is doing just fine, thank you very much. Where we're seeing the struggles is more in the manufacturing base, which has been hit very hard by these sector -- these 232 sectoral tariffs, things like steel, aluminum, autos. So different parts of the economy are feeling different impacts here.

I keep coming back to this every time you have me on. But I think the single biggest thing -- notwithstanding what's occurring in the Middle East right now -- the single most important economic driver for Canada this year is going to be this USMCA renegotiation. So I think the thing that's been holding a lot of Canadian businesses back from making forward-looking decisions around, where do I build this next factory, how many workers do I need to hire, do I add an extra shift? It's all been around this uncertainty of, what is the USMCA going to look like, or is it going to exist after this set of renegotiations that are ongoing today?

Greg Bonnell: So if we can take ourselves out of the here and now, not that we should for a long period of time, but just try to take a long-term look at where we might be headed by the end of this year, will you and I be sitting here in December talking about a TSX that has performed well, maybe even outperformed again?

Michael O'Brien: The building blocks are there. We could absolutely see that scenario. Like I say, there was a decent amount of momentum in the Canadian economy. I would also say there was a building sense of momentum in the US economy, which we've seen more recently with some of the PMI data being quite strong.

And again, on a bit of a reflationary impetus to this as well, which net-net benefits a lot of the Canadian sectors, if you can remove the uncertainty from USMCA and if what transpires in the Middle East is more on the bullish side of outcomes -- in other words, a relatively quick resolution, relatively short-term disruptions to energy flows -- then I think the building blocks are there for another good year in Canada and Canadian equities.

Original Post

data.title: BillionToOne: Delivering And Lagging At The Same Time
data.description:
I have been covering shares of BillionToOne, Inc. (BLLN) since its public offering, as its vision, its products, and its operating momentum have triggered my real interest from the outset.

Its cutting-edge molecular diagnostics for prenatal and oncology testing has been driving not just impressive growth but actually allowed the business to become profitable as well. Strategic agreements and positive research results boosted momentum on top of this, although the business remains largely a one-trick pony.

Having ended the year on a strong note, the company calls for more growth in 2026. This outlook feels conservative, which, in combination with the setback in the share price, makes me willing to increase my exposure here.

Ending 2025 On A Solid Note

Early in March, BillionToOne reported a 113% increase in fourth-quarter sales to $96.1 million. This momentum is driven by the prenatal clinical testing business, growing sales some 99% to $86.1 million. This is largely complemented by a $9.1 million revenue contribution from oncology clinical testing, a number increasing by a factor of 8 times on the year before.

Growth is driven by a combination of pricing and the number of tests performed, the latter increasing some 47% on the year before, as average selling prices have been strong as well.

Digging into the results, the oncology sales growth has been a bit soft, as a $9.1 million revenue number in the fourth quarter, even while increasing by a factor of 8 times year-over-year, was up just about 5% on a sequential basis. It should be said that oncology sales have been lumpier in the past and that overall sales growth came in a bit stronger than expected.

Strong growth and high gross margins made it so that quarterly GAAP operating profits just surpassed $10 million, for low double-digit margins. The company reported full-year operating profits of $16 million on $305 million in sales.

Having previously already guided for 2026 sales to come in between $415 and $430 million, this guidance was hiked by $430 to $445 million. On average that works out to around $110 million per quarter, suggesting continuous sequential improvements, although it still feels as if the guidance might be conservative.

Moreover, the company launched an expanded Red Blood Cell Fetal Antigen (NIPT) offering as well as a Platelet Fetal Antigen (NIPT) offering. Besides, the Northstar PGx and Select CH tests were launched for oncology products, although no individual expectations have been provided for these products, although they might help to diversify away from the prenatal test revenues.

About Expectations

Trading down to $75 per share following the release of the results, the 39.4 million shares (on a diluted basis) represent a $2.95 billion equity valuation. This includes about $440 million in net cash holdings; as adjusted for that, operating assets are valued at around $2.5 billion.

With revenues seen around $437.5 million this year, forward sales multiples are seen at just 5.7 times sales. This follows >40% sales growth anticipated this year, as that feels potentially conservative.

Do Investors Have The Fear Of The Unknown?

The mission of BillionToOne was to remove the fear of the unknown with technologies designed to push beyond limitations in diagnostics, with the ultimate goal of being able to detect diseases with just a single molecule.

The business has been developed on the basic question of what is the most challenging and most impactful global healthcare problem. BillionToOne aims to create actionable insights from single blood draws, which not just provide actionable results but also offer personalized recommendations as well across a range of indications.

Following the IPO in November of last year, shares were priced at $60 per share but jumped to $120 on the first day of trading, now having given up much of the initial gains, trading at levels in the mid-seventies. Part of this comes as the company sees growth slowing down, as triple-digit growth rates have come down to double-digit percentages.

What Is Next?

In between a solid fourth quarter and a solid 2026 guidance (being hiked by fifteen million on the year before), I believe that the company is actually guiding conservatively, all while expectations come down further. Moreover, the company reached an important deal with Johnson & Johnson (JNJ) towards year-end 2025 in hemolytic diseases of fetuses and newborns, while some positive research results have been announced as well.

At that point, that was enough for me to hold a modest long position towards year-end, as I was looking to learn more about the 2026 performance. Looking into the year, I conclude that the fourth quarter results were some kind of mixed bag. While the headline numbers for the fourth quarter looked good, pricing was a big part of this, with sequential volume growth from the third quarter up just 3%, and the oncology franchise saw little sequential growth.

However, the conference call provided comfort to me as the outlook for the year looks somewhat deliberately soft, as the company calls out the fact that the guidance does not really factor in a contribution from recent launches. Moreover, the company touts a coverage deal with United Healthcare (UNH), which does not necessarily increase the number of covered lives but really reduces friction to access between patients and caregivers.

All this makes me feel comfortable in the sense that the guidance is conservative, and with profitability now achieved, earnings multiples remain high, but the real operational leverage potential is there. This makes me continue to be upbeat, as I am dipping my toes a bit further into the water, adding to a small speculative position.

This follows a substantial re-rating in the sales multiples, the operating leverage displayed, and the expectation that growth will surprise on the upside.

Similar high-growth stories are covered at Value In Corporate Events.

data.title: Surging Oil Prices May Be About To Jolt Markets
data.description:
If one thing seems clear, it is that oil, rates, and the dollar are all not only heading higher but have probably only just started their ascent. If this continues, the implications for the stock market could be significant as financial conditions tighten and dollar funding costs rise.

The conflict in Iran has awakened the sleepy oil market that has failed to participate in the commodity boom seen in precious metals. Should oil prices (CL1:COM) (CO1:COM) continue to rise, it would send ripple effects across the market, which could be substantial. Since 2022, interest rates have been highly correlated with oil prices, suggesting rates are likely to follow oil prices higher. A stronger dollar (DXY) is likely to follow.

Economic Data Moves To The Side

Even though the February job report was an epic disaster, showing a decline of 92,000 jobs, the 10-year rate (US10Y) is still rising by 3 bps as of 10 AM ET on March 6. That is because the 10-year rate is more closely tied to oil prices, since the market knows that much of the moderation in inflation over the past four years is due to a sharp decline in energy prices, suggesting the market now sees oil's disinflationary effects as largely over.

Oil has not only seen a solid rise in its price, but WTI Crude has broken out above $88, and technically, a push higher could lead to a further breakout that might send oil prices well into the mid-$90s. Additionally, the chart shows that a golden cross has formed, which is when the 50-day moving average rises above the 200-day moving average; this typically serves as a bullish indicator.

That said, oil has seen its relative strength index rise above 70 and the price rise above the upper Bollinger Band, which together can be a sign of overbought conditions. We have seen this overbought condition in precious metals several times, and in those cases, the metal often consolidated sideways for some time before making another move higher.

Rates And The Dollar At Risk

While risks to oil going higher are real, the risk to rates going even higher is even greater. Rates, by comparison to the move in oil, have not moved nearly as much, and that is probably because the market is trying to determine whether oil prices are making a sustainable or unsustainable move higher.

But what is also clear is that 10-year rates, and rates more broadly, have moved very closely with the price of oil since 2022, and if oil prices continue to rise or even just flatten out, it is likely to drag rates higher as well, even as risks of stagflation or an economic slowdown remain on the horizon.

This is very much true for the dollar index as well, and so what we are seeing right now in the market is a rare combination of the oil, rates, and the dollar all rising at the same time. The danger, of course, is if they continue to rise or stay elevated for some time, because together they will work to tighten financial conditions and lead to an economic slowdown as that happens.

The Spillover Into Stocks

This can be problematic for stocks, too, because as the dollar gains strength, the demand for dollar funding costs is likely to increase, and cross-currency basis swaps are likely to become more negative, making it more expensive to hedge the U.S. dollar. That is already happening with the USDJPY (USD:JPY) 5-year cross-currency basis swap, and historically, the stock market has followed this spread very closely, so as the basis becomes more negative, it will put more pressure on equities. On top of that, the tightening of financial conditions does not play well for equity markets either.

At this point, oil is in the driver's seat, and the longer oil stays elevated, the more pressure it will place on markets and the US economy.
